title: Has '1917' Already Locked Up Best Picture? And Is That Bad for the Academy Awards? | The Oscars Show
format: Recording from Sundance, Sean, Amanda, and Chris Ryan decide the Oscar race is already over after 1917's DGA win, run through the weekend box office, get into Guy Ritchie's The Gentlemen, and file dispatches from the festival.

segments.heading: Is the Oscar race already over?

films.note: Sam Mendes' DGA win (on top of the PGA and Globes) makes it, for the hosts, a Best Picture and Best Director lock. They read it as a technical-achievement war movie that peaked at the right moment on a compressed calendar, and the candidate with the fewest negatives: nobody has landed a case against it the way people have with past winners.

films.title: Parasite
films.note: Bong Joon-ho's SAG ensemble winner, the film the hosts are rooting for. Sean calls it 'the Hurt Locker of 2020,' though both it and 1917 are the year's dueling war-adjacent pictures. A likely International Feature winner; the open question is whether the Academy hands it a screenplay.

films.title: The Irishman
films.note: A late-December contender the hosts suspect voters are relieved not to have to rewatch.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: Once looked like Tarantino's coronation; now maybe running fourth. Screenplay is no longer a lock; it's been around a long time, which may be working against it.

films.title: Ford v Ferrari
films.note: The 'Christmas dad movie' Sean imagines swapping calendar slots with 1917 in a what-if: the old-world dad movie the Academy supposedly moved on from, only for 1917 to slide into the same lane.

films.title: Marriage Story
films.note: Noah Baumbach floated as a possible original screenplay upset.

films.title: Jojo Rabbit
films.note: Taika Waititi named as the adapted-screenplay threat to Greta Gerwig; Scarlett Johansson's second nomination of the year is here.

films.title: Little Women
films.note: Greta Gerwig took the USC Scripter, positioning her as the adapted-screenplay favorite. Saoirse Ronan is a Best Actress nominee.

films.title: Judy
films.note: Renée Zellweger is the presumed Best Actress winner. Sean's example of an awards movie that 'exists in an aquarium': lauded by voters, absent from any real-world conversation.

films.title: Bombshell
films.note: Charlize Theron's Best Actress nomination.

films.title: Harriet
films.note: Cynthia Erivo's Best Actress nomination, part of a slate the hosts note is short on actors of color.

films.title: Klaus
films.note: The Annie Awards' surprise Best Animated Feature winner. Hand-drawn, which the hosts flag as notable; Sean strings out a running bit pretending it's about Santa Claus versus legal clauses.

films.title: I Lost My Body
films.note: Took the Annie in the indie category and is also an Oscar animated-feature nominee, more evidence that the animation race, unlike everything else, has no consensus.

films.title: Toy Story 4
films.note: Sean's prior pick for the Oscar animated feature; it lost the Annie, leaving that category up in the air.

films.title: American Factory
films.note: Steven Bognar and Julia Reichert's DGA documentary winner, and the presumed Best Documentary front-runner.

films.title: Moonlight
films.note: The time the Academy got it right, and the hope the hosts admit they keep reinvesting: it beat La La Land after Damien Chazelle took Best Director in a split.

films.title: La La Land
films.note: Amanda's example of a movie she could finally articulate disliking, the kind of clarity nobody's managed against 1917.

films.title: Gravity
films.note: Alfonso Cuarón won Best Director but not Best Picture. The hosts note that split is common, the kind of achievement movie that doesn't always win the top prize.

films.title: Avatar
films.note: The last technical-marvel juggernaut, a huge deal to filmmakers at the time, that still lost Best Picture.

films.title: The Hurt Locker
films.note: The war movie that beat Avatar; Sean's template for how Parasite could theoretically topple 1917.

films.title: Argo
films.note: The lone DGA exception: Ben Affleck won it, then wasn't even nominated for the directing Oscar, the outlier that keeps 15-of-16 from being 16-of-16.

segments.heading: Stock up, stock down: the box office

films.title: Bad Boys for Life
films.note: A mega hit, and the hosts' theory for why: the IP is Will Smith and Martin Lawrence's chemistry, not a mythology. There's no Silmarillion of Mike Lowry. Kate del Castillo (the actress who led Sean Penn to El Chapo) plays the witch, La Bruja.

films.title: Terminator: Dark Fate
films.note: Cited as a retread franchise revival that flopped, 'okay, not bad,' but not Bad Boys.

films.title: Men in Black: International
films.note: Another failed Will Smith-adjacent revival; Sean calls it 'apocalyptically bad.'

films.title: Charlie's Angels
films.note: The other revival that didn't work; Sean hadn't seen it, so no verdict.

films.title: Dolittle
films.note: Surprisingly did not die: kids turned out for talking animals. Setup for the running bit about Stephen Gaghan's troubles directing digital-animal-human interaction.

films.title: Syriana
films.note: Stephen Gaghan's earlier film, invoked in a mock Rogan-appearance bit about what drew Robert Downey Jr. to Dolittle.

segments.heading: Chris Ryan on 'The Gentlemen'
segments.note: Guy Ritchie's return to the gangster mode

films.title: The Gentlemen
films.note: Chris admits it's regressive, kind of racist, and pretty stupid, and that he really liked it: a smart-dumb British gangster movie, staged almost like a play in long dialogue scenes. Wild swings from Hugh Grant, Matthew McConaughey ('thinks he's in a Lincoln commercial'), Colin Farrell (a tartan-clad Irish gym coach, Chris's favorite thing in ages), Charlie Hunnam, Henry Golding, Michelle Dockery, and Jeremy Strong. Financed partly by the new Miramax in 2018, bought by STX for around $7M, and made about $11M opening weekend.

films.title: Lock, Stock and Two Smoking Barrels
films.note: The stylish, knowingly stupid British gangster mode Ritchie made his name on and is returning to.

films.title: Snatch
films.note: The other Ritchie touchstone in that original run.

films.title: King Arthur: Legend of the Sword
films.note: Part of the 'IP Gulag' Ritchie spent five years in before this.

films.title: Aladdin
films.note: The other big studio-IP job on Ritchie's recent résumé.

films.title: Sherlock Holmes
films.note: The franchise work that preceded the IP stretch.

films.title: Triple Frontier
films.note: The Charlie Hunnam character Chris says he relates to most, the one who chokes a man out in a Publix.

films.title: The Lost City of Z
films.note: Chris's example of Hunnam's booming, Shakespearean-projection voice work.

films.title: Tropic Thunder
films.note: Source of the 'a guy playing a guy' Robert Downey Jr. line the hosts apply to Hunnam.

films.title: Last Christmas
films.note: Henry Golding's other recent title. It made money in the UK, so his post-Crazy Rich Asians run is 'hanging on.'

films.title: Crazy Rich Asians
films.note: Golding's breakout, the baseline against which his stardom is being measured.

films.title: Ocean's Eleven
films.note: The charming-crooks counterexample: The Gentlemen is not that; it's two hours of cockney swearing.

films.title: First Reformed
films.note: The Gentlemen opens with blood splashing into a beer, which Chris reads as the 'low-rent version' of the Pepto-Bismol-in-whiskey image, a movie the hosts wish they were still talking about this awards season.

films.title: Pulp Fiction
films.note: The permission structure the movie seems nostalgic for: when films could 'be that.'

films.title: Shirley
films.note: Josephine Decker's claustrophobic film about Shirley Jackson (Elizabeth Moss) writing a novel. Amanda pitched it as The Secret History but about women; both liked it, Sean less so after watching it from the front row, straight up Moss's nostrils. An incredible ending.

films.title: The Night House
films.note: David Bruckner's horror film with Rebecca Hall as a widow investigating her husband's suicide. Chris admired it but saw it stacking three genres at once, a product of 'I can't believe we got to make this,' with a Pacific Heights element, a Shining element, and loud jump scares.

films.title: The Ritual
films.note: Bruckner's earlier Netflix horror movie, Chris's reference point for him.

films.title: Dick Johnson Is Dead
films.note: Sean's favorite of the festival so far: Kirsten Johnson staging her aging father's deaths on camera. Funny, peppy, heart-rending, 89 minutes, and headed to Netflix; he'd bet on it for Best Documentary a year out.

films.title: Cameraperson
films.note: Kirsten Johnson's impressionistic earlier documentary, widely called one of the best of the decade.

films.title: Frozen (2010)
films.note: The ski-lift survival thriller, invoked when the Sundance chairlift becomes a running gag, a woman stranded on the far side of the mountain.

films: The Lord of the Rings: The Two Towers

Once Upon a Time in Hollywood.title: Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood

Lock, Stock and Two Smoking Barrels.title: Lock, Stock and Two Smoking Barrels

King Arthur: Legend of the Sword.title: King Arthur: Legend of the Sword

The Lord of the Rings: The Two Towers.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Two Towers
